subtitle: In the Interest of Science—and for Humanity!
summary: Walter Reed leaves for Washington, entrusting Carroll and Lazear with secret, daring mosquito experiments in Cuba. After initial failures, James Carroll volunteers to be bitten by the most dangerous infected mosquito and nearly dies of yellow fever, becoming the first deliberate experimental case. A private soldier called X.Y. also falls ill after mosquito bites. Then tragedy strikes: a stray mosquito bites Jesse Lazear in the yellow fever ward, and he dies of the disease on September 25, 1900.

segments.anchor_sentence: Then Walter Reed (he had been called home to Washington to make a report on work done in the Spanish War) gave elaborate instructions to Carroll and Lazear and Agramonte.

visual.scene_description: Lazear moves between beds of yellow fever patients in a military hospital ward, carefully holding glass tubes containing silver-striped mosquitoes against the arms of sick soldiers.

segments.anchor_sentence: But look at the bold face of Jesse Lazear, and tell me if that was a patient man!

visual.scene_description: James Carroll, a tough middle-aged soldier, sits with his sleeve rolled up as Lazear carefully places a mosquito on his bare forearm. Carroll watches stoically as the insect prepares to bite.

segments.anchor_sentence: That soldier watched her while she felt around with her stinger....

visual.scene_description: Carroll lies gravely ill in a hospital bed with bloodshot eyes and flushed face, while Lazear stands beside him checking a thermometer. Carroll survived and later called these his proudest days.

segments.anchor_sentence: Then there was that American private soldier they called "X.Y."—these outlaw searchers called him "X.Y.," though he was really William Dean, of Grand Rapids, Michigan.

visual.scene_description: Lazear feeds mosquitoes in the yellow fever ward while a stray mosquito lands unnoticed on the back of his hand. He dismisses it as harmless, not knowing it will seal his fate.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a young man in shirtsleeves holds inverted glass tubes against a patient's arm in a dim hospital ward, a single stray silver-striped mosquito perches on the back of his other hand feeding unnoticed, rows of iron beds with sick soldiers recede into shadow, louvered wooden shutters filter pale tropical light across the floor

segments.anchor_sentence: "On the evening of September 18th ... Dr. Lazear complained of feeling out of sorts, and had a chill at 8 P.M.," says a hospital record of Las Animas....

visual.scene_description: Lazear lies dying in the same yellow fever ward where he once collected mosquitoes, his face jaundiced, while a fellow doctor records his declining vital signs in a ledger.
